outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4084:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:285:      "content": "Solar Energy Materials & Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\nAvailable online 20 October 2023\n0927-0248/© 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).\nEffect of global damp heat ageing on debonding of crosslinked EVA- and \nPOE-glass laminates \nMartin Tiefenthaler a,b,*, Gernot M. Wallner a,b, Robert Pugstaller a,b \na Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, Austria \nb Institute of Polymeric Materials and Testing, University of Linz, Austria  \nA B S T R A C T   \nPolyolefin elastomers (POE) have been established as a promising alternative to commonly used peroxide crosslinked ethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) \nphotovoltaic encapsulation materials. The main objective of this work was to assess the long-term ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV-transparent \nEVA and POE under damp heat (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) conditions for up to 10,000h. Ageing induced changes were characterized by microscopy, UV–visible–near infrared \n(UVVISNIR) spectroscopy, compressive shear testing (CST), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), macro- and microscopic Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X- \nray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Within the laminates, the formation of birefringent spheric microdomains (maltese-crosses) was confirmed after an exposure \ntime of more than 2,000h. The compressive shear behavior of EVA laminates was not significantly affected by damp heat ageing. Interestingly, the POE laminates \nrevealed an even improved compressive shear performance. Whereas the tested EVA laminates failed in an interfacial manner independent on damp heat ageing time, \nPOE laminates exhibited a failure mode transition from interfacial (unaged) to combined interfacial and cohesive (10,000h). On the interfacially fractured surfaces of \nEVA and POE laminates, carboxylic acid groups were ascertained. Deprotonated silanol and water bands were discernible on the opposite glass sides. Damp heat \nexposure of POE laminates for 10,000h resulted in pronounced glass ageing close to the interface associated with a partly cohesive failure within glass. This phe­\nnomenon was quite different to EVA and was attributed to the alkaline characteristics of the hindered amine light (HALS) stabilized, less-polar, UV-transparent POE \nencapsulant.   \n1. Introduction \nThe lifetime and power output of photovoltaic (PV) modules depend \nsignificantly on the polymeric encapsulation material [1–6]. Ethylene \nvinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) is still dominating the market. Field aged \nPV modules with EVA encapsulation show signs of delamination, yel­\nlowing and potential induced degradation (PID) [5–8]. Conventional \nglobal ageing and delamination characterization consisting of first, \nenvironmental exposure of laminates without external mechanical \nloads, and second, monotonic mechanical testing is rather time \nconsuming and of limited service-relevance. Thus, novel local ageing \napproaches based on fatigue testing of double-glass laminates under \nsuperimposed environmental influences and mechanical stresses are \nunder development [3,9,10]. Both, global and local ageing character­\nization methods have confirmed diffusion-controlled degradation \nmechanisms at the interface of the glass substrate and the encapsulation \nmaterial [3,11–13]. \nThe polarity of EVA facilitates water diffusion leading to degradation \nmechanisms such as deacetylation, β-scission and hydrolytic depoly­\nmerization [11,14–16]. Poor barrier properties of EVA against ion \nmigration from the front glass to the silicon cell are responsible for \npotential induced degradation of PV modules [17]. Therefore, current \nresearch and material development is focused on less-polar substitution \nmaterials based on polyolefin elastomers (POE) [4,18–20]. \nBarretta et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat (DH, 85 ◦C, 85 \n%rh) exposure on the performance of crosslinked EVA and POE films. No \nsignificant changes of optical transmittance as well as surface and bulk \nchemistry were detected after damp heat exposure for up to 3,000h. \nOreski et al. [18] examined the influence of DH on the degradation and \npower output of 6-cell PV modules with EVA or POE encapsulants. \nAcetic acid induced degradation of the cells encapsulated with EVA was \nconfirmed after 3,000h, whereas no significant changes were observed \nin POE-based modules. \nThe exposure of glass substrates to DH conditions triggers ageing \nmechanisms such as leaching of network cations (e.g., Na, Ca), hydro­\nlysis of the Si–O network, glass corrosion and carbonate deposition on \nthe surfaces [21]. Hence, global ageing of glass substrates and its \ninteraction with the encapsulant has also an impact on the lifetime of PV \nmodules [21–24]. Silane based coupling agents improve the adhesion \nand the resistance against DH induced degradation of EVA-glass \n* Corresponding author. Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, \nAustria. \nE-mail address: martin.tiefenthaler@jku.at (M. Tiefenthaler).  \nContents lists available at ScienceDirect \nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells \njournal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/solmat \nhttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.solmat.2023.112602 \nReceived 10 May 2023; Received in revised form 26 September 2023; Accepted 16 October 2023   \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n2\nlaminates \n[13,25]. \nThe \nlong-term \nbehavior \nof \ncovalent \nencapsulant-Si-O-Si-glass bonds is crucial for the long-term delamina­\ntion resistance of PV modules [13,25]. The EVA-glass interface is more \nprone to ageing induced delamination than the EVA-silicon cell interface \n[11]. In the early stage, the degradation mechanism of the EVA-glass \ninterface is characterized by deacetylation and β-scission reactions of \nEVA and later on by hydrolytic depolymerization [11]. Furthermore, \nsodium ion migration plays a major role associated with a significantly \nenhanced sodium content on the fractured interface [11]. \nIn a recent paper, it was clearly shown that the degradation mech­\nanisms within double-glass modules are highly dependent on the \nformulation of the encapsulation material [12]. A variety of commer­\ncially relevant and experimental encapsulation formulations has been \ninvestigated without clear differentiation and description of the stabi­\nlizer package used. Moreover, compressive shear testing subsequent to \nenvironmental exposure of double-glass laminates is gaining importance \nfor delamination characterization [26–30]. \nHence, the main objective of this paper was to evaluate and compare \nthe damp heat ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV- \ntransparent EVA and POE encapsulants. The UV-transparency is ach­\nieved by adding just hindered amine light stabilizers (HALS) which gives \nthe encapsulation material more alkaline characteristics. Phenolic an­\ntioxidants, which are prone to the formation of acidic low molar mass \ndegradation products due to the ester-based linkage groups, were \navoided. Quadratic compressive shear double-glass specimens were \nlaminated and exposed for up to 10,000 h to damp heat. Subsequently \nand prior to mechanical testing, non-destructive microscopic and \nUVVISNIR spectroscopic analysis was carried out. Then, the mechanical \nbehavior was examined by compressive shear testing. Finally, the frac­\ntured surfaces and the remaining encapsulant were analyzed by differ­\nential scanning calorimetry (DSC), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) \nspectroscopy/microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). \n2. Experimental \n2.1. Materials and laminates \nTwo peroxide crosslinking UV-transparent encapsulants based on \nethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) \nwere investigated. While the EVA film revealed a non-periodic topog­\nraphy, the POE had a periodic pyramidal topography. A non-thermally \ntoughened solar glass allowing for water jet cutting after lamination \nwas used in this study. By XPS, the following composition of the glass \nsurface was determined (in at%): 21.1 Si, 57.5 O, 11.1 C, 2.6 N, 5.7 Na, \n1.3 Ca and 0.7 K. Details on the chemical composition and the ther­\nmomechanical properties of the encapsulants are given in Ref. [19]. \nTwo EVA or POE films were used to prepare double-glass laminates \n(300 × 200 × 3.1 mm3) at 155 ◦C, 800 mbar for 20 min. Double-glass \nspecimens with dimensions of 25 × 25 × 7 mm3 were prepared by \nwater jet cutting. Specimens were damp heat exposed (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) in \nan environmental chamber WK3-340/40 (Weiss Umwelttechnik GmbH, \nGermany) for 0, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000 and 10,000h. Whereas 3 specimens \nwere examined for each ageing interval, only 1 specimen was available \nafter 10,000h of damp heat exposure. \n2.2. Characterization methods \nPrior to compressive shear testing, optical and structural changes \nwere assessed by direct and polarized light microscopy (DLM and PLM) \nusing a VHX 7000 (Keyence, Japan). While DLM measurements were \nperformed using full ring incident light, PLM measurements were car­\nried out in transmittance mode with the specimen placed between two \nfully crossed (0/90◦) polarizers. DLM images were taken from the \nspecimens immediately after removal from the damp heat chamber. \nThen, the glass surfaces of the specimen were polished using an auto­\nmated wet-polishing unit with fine sandpapers. Hemispheric and diffuse \ntransmittance spectra in the range from 250 to 2500 nm were recorded \nin normal transmittance mode in 5 nm steps on a Lamda 950 UVVISNIR \nspectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 150 mm integrating \nsphere. The UV cut-off lengths were defined by a 10% drop in trans­\nmittance compared to the reference at 800 nm [31]. To deduce integral \nnormal-hemispheric and -diffuse transmittance values, the spectral data \nwere weighted with an AM1.5 source function [31,32]. The trans­\nmittance data were integrated in the solar (300–2000 nm) and UV \n(300–400 nm) range. Moreover, solar haze values were evaluated by \nnormalization of the diffuse with the hemispheric transmittance [33]. In \naddition to the laminates, the used glass substrate was characterized in \nthe unaged, reference state. \nShear failure is a common phenomenon related to interfacial ageing. \nWhile interlaminar shear testing methods are often applied for charac­\nterization of composites [34], in this study focus was given to \ncompressive shear testing as developed by Chapuis et al. [26] for double \nglass laminates. Compressive shear tests were carried out in tensile \nmode on an universal tensile testing machine Z020 (ZwickRoell, Ger­\nmany) equipped with a temperature chamber (see Fig. 1). The \ndisplacement rate was 1 mm/min. To prevent fracture of the glass \nsubstrate and to mimic critical service-relevant conditions, the test \ntemperature was set to 60 ◦C [27,29]. Shear loads detected by \ncompressive shear testing of double-glass laminates are at least 50 times \nhigher than service-relevant shear loads in a PV module [35]. Hence, the \nmain purpose of monotonic shear testing was to deduce ultimate me­\nchanical values allowing for quantitative comparison of the damp heat \nageing behavior of glass laminates based on the investigated \nUV-transparent EVA and POE encapsulants. Moreover, it should be \nevaluated if damp heat ageing for up to 10,000 h is associated with a \ncritical drop in ultimate shear loads below service-relevant shear \nloading conditions. \nAs illustrated in Fig. 2, the failure modes were classified as interfa­\ncial, cohesive in the encapsulant or glass and combined interfacial and \ncohesive. Remaining encapsulants on the fractured double-glass lami­\nnates were investigated by direct light microscopy (DLM). Moreover, the \ncorresponding glass sides were examined with a 3D laser confocal mi­\ncroscope LEXT OLS4000 (Olympus, Japan). Stitched 10x images of the \nentire glass surface (25 × 25 mm2) were generated. Details of the frac­\ntured surfaces and height profiles were deduced from 50x images. \nFurthermore, encapsulant samples were taken with a scalpel and \ncharacterized by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 8500, Perki­\nnElmer, USA). Samples (< 5 mg) were placed in a perforated 3 μL \naluminum pan. Thermograms were recorded with a heating/cooling rate \nof 20 K/min under nitrogen atmosphere. The experiments consisted of a \nfirst heating step (0–200 ◦C) followed by a cooling step (200-0 ◦C) and a \nsubsequent second heating step (0–200 ◦C). During damp heat ageing, \nthe edges of the encapsulant were directly exposed to water vapor. In \nFig. 1. Compressive shear test (CST) setup: a) schematic illustration according \nto Ref. [26]; b) image of setup implemented at an universal mechanical testing \nsystem equipped with a temperature chamber. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n3\ncontrast, the ingress to the center of the laminate was diffusion \ncontrolled. To assess potential structural differences within the encap­\nsulant layer, DSC samples were taken from the edge and the center. \nFTIR spectroscopy was carried out in attenuated total reflection \n(ATR) mode using a Spectrum 100 (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a \ndiamond/ZnSe ATR unit. The spectra were obtained by averaging 4 \nscans ranging from 650 to 4000 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 4 \ncm−1. For each encapsulant and exposure time, global-ATR spectra were \ncollected from the remaining encapsulant layer. Moreover, spatially \nresolved, local FTIR-ATR microscopic images with dimensions of 300 ×\n300 μm2 were recorded using a Spectrum 100 and a Spotlight 400 im­\naging system (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a Ge-crystal. Spectra \nwere run in the range from 720 to 4000 cm−1 with a spatial resolution of \n1.56 μm and a spectral resolution of 16 cm−1. The IR-ATR spectra were \nbaseline corrected. \nX-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were per­\nformed using a Theta Probe device and an Avantage data system \n(Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The chemical composition of fractured \nsurfaces was deduced from XPS survey scans with an Al-Kα mono­\nchromatic X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and a spot size diameter of 300 or \n400 μm. The pass energy of the hemispherical analyzer was set to 50 eV. \nFurther details as to XPS analysis are given in Ref. [36]. Special focus \nwas given to the analysis and changes of the sodium and calcium con­\ntents within the bulk encapsulant and at surfaces and interfaces of EVA \nand POE based double-glass laminates. To improve the comparability \nand decrease the effect of contaminations, measured sodium and cal­\ncium fractions were normalized by the corresponding silicon content. \nFTIR and XPS are differing significantly in their depth resolution. \nWhile a sample layer with a thickness of about 5 μm is assessed by FTIR \nin ATR-mode, XPS is a highly surface-sensitive characterization method \nwith a penetration depth of about 10 nm [37]. Hence, FTIR-ATR and XPS \nmeasurements were carried out to differentiate between ageing phe­\nnomena close to the fractured surface (by XPS) and within the usually \nplastically deformed damage zone within a few μm-distance to fractured \nsurface (by FTIR-ATR). \n3. Results and discussion \n3.1. Visual appearance and optical properties \nA silicate glass network contains bridging oxygens (BO, i.e., Si–O–Si) \nand non-bridging oxygens (NBO, e.g., Si–O-). The latter are charge \ncompensated by glass network modifier cations (e.g., Na, Ca). As well \ndiscussed in literature [21,22,24], DH exposure of silicate glass leads to \nthe formation of a surface water film and to diffusion of molecular water \ninto the bulk. The diffused water reacts with both, BO and NBO, asso­\nciated with 1) the formation of silanol groups, 2) the release of (glass) \nnetwork modifier cations, and 3) the formation of more NBO (Si–O-) \ngroups [21,22,24]. These three mechanisms are weakening the glass \nnetwork [21,24]. The network modifier cations migrate to the water \nfilm and cause a raise of the pH value by changing the electrochemical \npotential between the glass surface and the bulk. At a pH value of 9, the \nmigrated ions form water-insoluble carbonate (e.g., Na2CO3, CaCO3) \nclusters associated with permanent whitening of the surface [21]. In \nFig. 3, DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE laminates are \nillustrated after 0, 5,000 and 10,000h of DH exposure. Leaching induced \nsurface whitening was clearly discernible after 5,000h. The entire glass \nsurfaces of both laminates revealed a homogenous white appearance \nafter 10,000h. The surface whitening effect was not dependent on the \nencapsulant material (as shown in Fig. 3). \nMicroscopic images of surface polished EVA and POE laminates ob­\ntained by DLM (1st row) and PLM (2nd and 3rd row) after 0, 5,000, and \n10,000h of DH exposure are depicted and compared in Fig. 4. DLM \nimages revealed an increased whitening for both encapsulant types after \nFig. 2. Failure modes of compressive shear tested double-glass laminates: 1) interfacial failure at the encapsulant-glass interface, 2) cohesive failure: a) within the \nencapsulant, b) within the glass substrate. \nFig. 3. DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE-glass laminates \nexposed to DH for 0, 5,000 and 10,000h. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n4\n5,000h of DH exposure. The edge clearing effect was more pronounced \nfor EVA laminates. In contrast, the edges of the POE laminates appeared \nfully opaque after 10,000h (see Fig. 4). After 5,000h of DH exposure, \nspheric microdomains (maltese-crosses, d < 100 μm) were clearly \ndiscernible in the polarized light microscopic (PLM) images of both \nlaminate types (see Fig. 4). The microdomains differed in appearance, \nsize and number. Compared to POE, EVA laminates exhibited less, but \nlarger microdomains, which were presumably related to water uptake \nand clustering. The edge clearing effect of EVA laminates was dependent \non conditioning time at 23 ◦C and 50 %rh, subsequent to DH exposure. \nThe microdomains within the POE laminates were smaller and more \nhomogenous (see Fig. 4). Harris et al. [38] reported the formation of \ncalcite (CaCO3) crystals and spherolithic superstructures resulting in \nmaltese-crosses in PLM images. Most likely, the finer microdomains in \nPOE laminates could be attributed to glass ageing induced formation of \nCaCO3 and Na2CO3 crystals at the POE-glass interface [21]. Interest­\ningly, these microdomains were nucleated along the edge lines of the \npyramidal topography of the extruded POE film. In addition to the \nmaltese-cross microdomains, distinct black spots with a diameter up to \n2 mm were also discernible in POE laminates after 5,000 and 10,000h of \nDH exposure. \nIn contrast to POE, which was reported to prevent effectively ion \nmigration [17], the higher polarity of EVA favors ion diffusion into the \nbulk of the encapsulant [3,11,21]. Moreover, the damp heat induced \nhydrolysis of EVA could facilitate the formation of acetic acid, which \nwould reduce the alkalinity of the pH at the EVA-glass interface. These \ntwo mechanisms were ascribed to hinder the formation of sodium or \ncalcium carbonates at EVA-glass interfaces by keeping the pH value well \nbelow 9. \nSolar transmittance spectra of a single glass substrate and the lami­\nnates are depicted in Fig. 5. The hemispheric transmittance spectrum of \nthe glass substrate revealed an UV cut-off at 330 nm. The UV cut-off of \nEVA laminates shifted from 345 to 385 nm after 10,000h of damp heat \nexposure. A cut-off shift from 355 to 400 nm was also confirmed for POE \nlaminates. The results of the double-glass EVA laminates are in agree­\nment with findings in Refs. [35,39]. Morse et al. [39] did not detect any \nsignificant changes of the UV cut-off for POE laminates exposed to 1.5 \nMJ/m2 of solar radiation at 85 ◦C for 4,000h. The deviations within this \nwork and the findings in Ref. [39] were attributed to different ageing \nconditions. Barret et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat expo­\nsure on free standing crosslinked POE films and reported a decrease of \nthe hemispheric transmittance with increasing ageing durations, which \nis also in agreement with findings of this study (see Figs. 5 and 6b)). The \nlower hemispheric transmittance values of POE-based glass laminates in \nthe unaged reference state is presumably related to a stronger index of \nrefraction difference of glass and POE associated with higher reflectivity \nat the interfaces. The diffuse transmittance was significantly higher for \nthe unaged POE laminate. More pronounced scattering of POE is pre­\nsumably related to a higher degree of crystallinity and a lower cross­\nlinking density [19]. The increase in scattering at lower wavelength \nvalues is a clear indicator for scattering domains such as crystal lamellae \nwith a thickness well below 300 nm. While the lamellae thickness of PE \ncrystals is below 10 nm, the lateral dimensions of crystal lamellae are \nseveral 100 nm and hence, contributing to scattering [31]. Both lami­\nnates revealed hydrocarbon (CH) related absorption bands at 2500 to \n2200, 1800 to 1650, 1400 and 1200 nm and a small carbonyl (C=O) \nFig. 4. Direct (1st row) and polarized (2nd and 3rd row) light microscopic images of glass surface polished EVA (left) and POE (right) laminates exposed to DH for 0, \n5,000, and 10,000h. \nFig. 5. Effect of damp heat exposure (10,000h) on the hemispheric and diffuse transmittance (Th and Td) of EVA- and POE-glass laminates.  \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n5\npeak at 2100 nm [31]. Moreover, damp heat aged laminates (for 10, \n000h) revealed an additional band attributed to molecular water in the \nrange from 1950 to 1900 nm [31]. Interestingly, this band was more \npronounced for POE than for EVA laminates. Presumably, a water rich \nlayer was formed in the near interface glass regions of POE laminates \ndue to excellent bulk barrier properties of POE. However, water diffu­\nsion rates were reported to be at least two magnitudes higher at the \ninterface of metal oxides and polymeric adhesives, such as epoxies \n[40–42]. Presumably, the edges of the periodic, pyramidic topography \nresulted in imperfections at the interface allowing for fast ingress of \nwater. \nNormalized hemispheric transmittance values within the solar \n(300–2000 nm) and the UV (300–400 nm) range of EVA and POE lam­\ninates are illustrated in Fig. 6a). Unaged EVA laminates revealed slightly \nhigher (+1.5%) hemispheric solar and UV transmittance values than \nPOE. Compared to unaged laminates, damp heat exposed (1,000h) EVA \nand POE specimens exhibited a decrease in the hemispheric trans­\nmittance by −3.6 and −1.1%, respectively. After 10,000h of damp heat \nageing, the hemispheric solar transmittance was significantly higher for \nEVA laminates. A pronounced drop in solar transmittance (by −5.8%) \nwas ascertained just for the POE laminate. Morse et al. [39] reported a \ndecrease of hemispheric transmittance of double-glass EVA laminates by \n−3% due to global ageing at 80 ◦C, 30 %rh for 180 days. In contrast, no \nchanges were observed for POE laminates [39]. The poorer performance \nof POE laminates in this study was presumably attributable to damp heat \ninduced glass and interface ageing effects. A decrease of the hemispheric \ntransmittance was also described for free standing and damp heat \nexposed EVA and POE films [4]. \nUnexposed EVA laminates revealed about 4.0% higher hemispheric \nUV transmittance values than POE specimens (see Fig. 6a)). Damp heat \nexposure for 10,000h caused a significant decrease (−19.0%) of the UV \ntransmittance for both, EVA and POE laminates. \nThe solar haze increased by a factor of 9 after already 1,000h of \ndamp heat exposure of EVA laminates. No significant further changes \nwere discernible at longer damp heat exposure up to 10,000h. Compared \nto unaged specimens, POE laminates exhibited 2x (1,000h) and 3x \n(10,000h) higher haze values after damp heat exposure. However, the \nincrease in haze of POE-glass was much less compared to EVA-glass. The \nageing induced haze of the laminates wa"
outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4106:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:285:      "content": "Solar Energy Materials & Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\nAvailable online 20 October 2023\n0927-0248/© 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).\nEffect of global damp heat ageing on debonding of crosslinked EVA- and \nPOE-glass laminates \nMartin Tiefenthaler a,b,*, Gernot M. Wallner a,b, Robert Pugstaller a,b \na Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, Austria \nb Institute of Polymeric Materials and Testing, University of Linz, Austria  \nA B S T R A C T   \nPolyolefin elastomers (POE) have been established as a promising alternative to commonly used peroxide crosslinked ethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) \nphotovoltaic encapsulation materials. The main objective of this work was to assess the long-term ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV-transparent \nEVA and POE under damp heat (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) conditions for up to 10,000h. Ageing induced changes were characterized by microscopy, UV–visible–near infrared \n(UVVISNIR) spectroscopy, compressive shear testing (CST), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), macro- and microscopic Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X- \nray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Within the laminates, the formation of birefringent spheric microdomains (maltese-crosses) was confirmed after an exposure \ntime of more than 2,000h. The compressive shear behavior of EVA laminates was not significantly affected by damp heat ageing. Interestingly, the POE laminates \nrevealed an even improved compressive shear performance. Whereas the tested EVA laminates failed in an interfacial manner independent on damp heat ageing time, \nPOE laminates exhibited a failure mode transition from interfacial (unaged) to combined interfacial and cohesive (10,000h). On the interfacially fractured surfaces of \nEVA and POE laminates, carboxylic acid groups were ascertained. Deprotonated silanol and water bands were discernible on the opposite glass sides. Damp heat \nexposure of POE laminates for 10,000h resulted in pronounced glass ageing close to the interface associated with a partly cohesive failure within glass. This phe­\nnomenon was quite different to EVA and was attributed to the alkaline characteristics of the hindered amine light (HALS) stabilized, less-polar, UV-transparent POE \nencapsulant.   \n1. Introduction \nThe lifetime and power output of photovoltaic (PV) modules depend \nsignificantly on the polymeric encapsulation material [1–6]. Ethylene \nvinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) is still dominating the market. Field aged \nPV modules with EVA encapsulation show signs of delamination, yel­\nlowing and potential induced degradation (PID) [5–8]. Conventional \nglobal ageing and delamination characterization consisting of first, \nenvironmental exposure of laminates without external mechanical \nloads, and second, monotonic mechanical testing is rather time \nconsuming and of limited service-relevance. Thus, novel local ageing \napproaches based on fatigue testing of double-glass laminates under \nsuperimposed environmental influences and mechanical stresses are \nunder development [3,9,10]. Both, global and local ageing character­\nization methods have confirmed diffusion-controlled degradation \nmechanisms at the interface of the glass substrate and the encapsulation \nmaterial [3,11–13]. \nThe polarity of EVA facilitates water diffusion leading to degradation \nmechanisms such as deacetylation, β-scission and hydrolytic depoly­\nmerization [11,14–16]. Poor barrier properties of EVA against ion \nmigration from the front glass to the silicon cell are responsible for \npotential induced degradation of PV modules [17]. Therefore, current \nresearch and material development is focused on less-polar substitution \nmaterials based on polyolefin elastomers (POE) [4,18–20]. \nBarretta et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat (DH, 85 ◦C, 85 \n%rh) exposure on the performance of crosslinked EVA and POE films. No \nsignificant changes of optical transmittance as well as surface and bulk \nchemistry were detected after damp heat exposure for up to 3,000h. \nOreski et al. [18] examined the influence of DH on the degradation and \npower output of 6-cell PV modules with EVA or POE encapsulants. \nAcetic acid induced degradation of the cells encapsulated with EVA was \nconfirmed after 3,000h, whereas no significant changes were observed \nin POE-based modules. \nThe exposure of glass substrates to DH conditions triggers ageing \nmechanisms such as leaching of network cations (e.g., Na, Ca), hydro­\nlysis of the Si–O network, glass corrosion and carbonate deposition on \nthe surfaces [21]. Hence, global ageing of glass substrates and its \ninteraction with the encapsulant has also an impact on the lifetime of PV \nmodules [21–24]. Silane based coupling agents improve the adhesion \nand the resistance against DH induced degradation of EVA-glass \n* Corresponding author. Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, \nAustria. \nE-mail address: martin.tiefenthaler@jku.at (M. Tiefenthaler).  \nContents lists available at ScienceDirect \nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells \njournal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/solmat \nhttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.solmat.2023.112602 \nReceived 10 May 2023; Received in revised form 26 September 2023; Accepted 16 October 2023   \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n2\nlaminates \n[13,25]. \nThe \nlong-term \nbehavior \nof \ncovalent \nencapsulant-Si-O-Si-glass bonds is crucial for the long-term delamina­\ntion resistance of PV modules [13,25]. The EVA-glass interface is more \nprone to ageing induced delamination than the EVA-silicon cell interface \n[11]. In the early stage, the degradation mechanism of the EVA-glass \ninterface is characterized by deacetylation and β-scission reactions of \nEVA and later on by hydrolytic depolymerization [11]. Furthermore, \nsodium ion migration plays a major role associated with a significantly \nenhanced sodium content on the fractured interface [11]. \nIn a recent paper, it was clearly shown that the degradation mech­\nanisms within double-glass modules are highly dependent on the \nformulation of the encapsulation material [12]. A variety of commer­\ncially relevant and experimental encapsulation formulations has been \ninvestigated without clear differentiation and description of the stabi­\nlizer package used. Moreover, compressive shear testing subsequent to \nenvironmental exposure of double-glass laminates is gaining importance \nfor delamination characterization [26–30]. \nHence, the main objective of this paper was to evaluate and compare \nthe damp heat ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV- \ntransparent EVA and POE encapsulants. The UV-transparency is ach­\nieved by adding just hindered amine light stabilizers (HALS) which gives \nthe encapsulation material more alkaline characteristics. Phenolic an­\ntioxidants, which are prone to the formation of acidic low molar mass \ndegradation products due to the ester-based linkage groups, were \navoided. Quadratic compressive shear double-glass specimens were \nlaminated and exposed for up to 10,000 h to damp heat. Subsequently \nand prior to mechanical testing, non-destructive microscopic and \nUVVISNIR spectroscopic analysis was carried out. Then, the mechanical \nbehavior was examined by compressive shear testing. Finally, the frac­\ntured surfaces and the remaining encapsulant were analyzed by differ­\nential scanning calorimetry (DSC), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) \nspectroscopy/microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). \n2. Experimental \n2.1. Materials and laminates \nTwo peroxide crosslinking UV-transparent encapsulants based on \nethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) \nwere investigated. While the EVA film revealed a non-periodic topog­\nraphy, the POE had a periodic pyramidal topography. A non-thermally \ntoughened solar glass allowing for water jet cutting after lamination \nwas used in this study. By XPS, the following composition of the glass \nsurface was determined (in at%): 21.1 Si, 57.5 O, 11.1 C, 2.6 N, 5.7 Na, \n1.3 Ca and 0.7 K. Details on the chemical composition and the ther­\nmomechanical properties of the encapsulants are given in Ref. [19]. \nTwo EVA or POE films were used to prepare double-glass laminates \n(300 × 200 × 3.1 mm3) at 155 ◦C, 800 mbar for 20 min. Double-glass \nspecimens with dimensions of 25 × 25 × 7 mm3 were prepared by \nwater jet cutting. Specimens were damp heat exposed (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) in \nan environmental chamber WK3-340/40 (Weiss Umwelttechnik GmbH, \nGermany) for 0, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000 and 10,000h. Whereas 3 specimens \nwere examined for each ageing interval, only 1 specimen was available \nafter 10,000h of damp heat exposure. \n2.2. Characterization methods \nPrior to compressive shear testing, optical and structural changes \nwere assessed by direct and polarized light microscopy (DLM and PLM) \nusing a VHX 7000 (Keyence, Japan). While DLM measurements were \nperformed using full ring incident light, PLM measurements were car­\nried out in transmittance mode with the specimen placed between two \nfully crossed (0/90◦) polarizers. DLM images were taken from the \nspecimens immediately after removal from the damp heat chamber. \nThen, the glass surfaces of the specimen were polished using an auto­\nmated wet-polishing unit with fine sandpapers. Hemispheric and diffuse \ntransmittance spectra in the range from 250 to 2500 nm were recorded \nin normal transmittance mode in 5 nm steps on a Lamda 950 UVVISNIR \nspectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 150 mm integrating \nsphere. The UV cut-off lengths were defined by a 10% drop in trans­\nmittance compared to the reference at 800 nm [31]. To deduce integral \nnormal-hemispheric and -diffuse transmittance values, the spectral data \nwere weighted with an AM1.5 source function [31,32]. The trans­\nmittance data were integrated in the solar (300–2000 nm) and UV \n(300–400 nm) range. Moreover, solar haze values were evaluated by \nnormalization of the diffuse with the hemispheric transmittance [33]. In \naddition to the laminates, the used glass substrate was characterized in \nthe unaged, reference state. \nShear failure is a common phenomenon related to interfacial ageing. \nWhile interlaminar shear testing methods are often applied for charac­\nterization of composites [34], in this study focus was given to \ncompressive shear testing as developed by Chapuis et al. [26] for double \nglass laminates. Compressive shear tests were carried out in tensile \nmode on an universal tensile testing machine Z020 (ZwickRoell, Ger­\nmany) equipped with a temperature chamber (see Fig. 1). The \ndisplacement rate was 1 mm/min. To prevent fracture of the glass \nsubstrate and to mimic critical service-relevant conditions, the test \ntemperature was set to 60 ◦C [27,29]. Shear loads detected by \ncompressive shear testing of double-glass laminates are at least 50 times \nhigher than service-relevant shear loads in a PV module [35]. Hence, the \nmain purpose of monotonic shear testing was to deduce ultimate me­\nchanical values allowing for quantitative comparison of the damp heat \nageing behavior of glass laminates based on the investigated \nUV-transparent EVA and POE encapsulants. Moreover, it should be \nevaluated if damp heat ageing for up to 10,000 h is associated with a \ncritical drop in ultimate shear loads below service-relevant shear \nloading conditions. \nAs illustrated in Fig. 2, the failure modes were classified as interfa­\ncial, cohesive in the encapsulant or glass and combined interfacial and \ncohesive. Remaining encapsulants on the fractured double-glass lami­\nnates were investigated by direct light microscopy (DLM). Moreover, the \ncorresponding glass sides were examined with a 3D laser confocal mi­\ncroscope LEXT OLS4000 (Olympus, Japan). Stitched 10x images of the \nentire glass surface (25 × 25 mm2) were generated. Details of the frac­\ntured surfaces and height profiles were deduced from 50x images. \nFurthermore, encapsulant samples were taken with a scalpel and \ncharacterized by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 8500, Perki­\nnElmer, USA). Samples (< 5 mg) were placed in a perforated 3 μL \naluminum pan. Thermograms were recorded with a heating/cooling rate \nof 20 K/min under nitrogen atmosphere. The experiments consisted of a \nfirst heating step (0–200 ◦C) followed by a cooling step (200-0 ◦C) and a \nsubsequent second heating step (0–200 ◦C). During damp heat ageing, \nthe edges of the encapsulant were directly exposed to water vapor. In \nFig. 1. Compressive shear test (CST) setup: a) schematic illustration according \nto Ref. [26]; b) image of setup implemented at an universal mechanical testing \nsystem equipped with a temperature chamber. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n3\ncontrast, the ingress to the center of the laminate was diffusion \ncontrolled. To assess potential structural differences within the encap­\nsulant layer, DSC samples were taken from the edge and the center. \nFTIR spectroscopy was carried out in attenuated total reflection \n(ATR) mode using a Spectrum 100 (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a \ndiamond/ZnSe ATR unit. The spectra were obtained by averaging 4 \nscans ranging from 650 to 4000 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 4 \ncm−1. For each encapsulant and exposure time, global-ATR spectra were \ncollected from the remaining encapsulant layer. Moreover, spatially \nresolved, local FTIR-ATR microscopic images with dimensions of 300 ×\n300 μm2 were recorded using a Spectrum 100 and a Spotlight 400 im­\naging system (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a Ge-crystal. Spectra \nwere run in the range from 720 to 4000 cm−1 with a spatial resolution of \n1.56 μm and a spectral resolution of 16 cm−1. The IR-ATR spectra were \nbaseline corrected. \nX-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were per­\nformed using a Theta Probe device and an Avantage data system \n(Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The chemical composition of fractured \nsurfaces was deduced from XPS survey scans with an Al-Kα mono­\nchromatic X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and a spot size diameter of 300 or \n400 μm. The pass energy of the hemispherical analyzer was set to 50 eV. \nFurther details as to XPS analysis are given in Ref. [36]. Special focus \nwas given to the analysis and changes of the sodium and calcium con­\ntents within the bulk encapsulant and at surfaces and interfaces of EVA \nand POE based double-glass laminates. To improve the comparability \nand decrease the effect of contaminations, measured sodium and cal­\ncium fractions were normalized by the corresponding silicon content. \nFTIR and XPS are differing significantly in their depth resolution. \nWhile a sample layer with a thickness of about 5 μm is assessed by FTIR \nin ATR-mode, XPS is a highly surface-sensitive characterization method \nwith a penetration depth of about 10 nm [37]. Hence, FTIR-ATR and XPS \nmeasurements were carried out to differentiate between ageing phe­\nnomena close to the fractured surface (by XPS) and within the usually \nplastically deformed damage zone within a few μm-distance to fractured \nsurface (by FTIR-ATR). \n3. Results and discussion \n3.1. Visual appearance and optical properties \nA silicate glass network contains bridging oxygens (BO, i.e., Si–O–Si) \nand non-bridging oxygens (NBO, e.g., Si–O-). The latter are charge \ncompensated by glass network modifier cations (e.g., Na, Ca). As well \ndiscussed in literature [21,22,24], DH exposure of silicate glass leads to \nthe formation of a surface water film and to diffusion of molecular water \ninto the bulk. The diffused water reacts with both, BO and NBO, asso­\nciated with 1) the formation of silanol groups, 2) the release of (glass) \nnetwork modifier cations, and 3) the formation of more NBO (Si–O-) \ngroups [21,22,24]. These three mechanisms are weakening the glass \nnetwork [21,24]. The network modifier cations migrate to the water \nfilm and cause a raise of the pH value by changing the electrochemical \npotential between the glass surface and the bulk. At a pH value of 9, the \nmigrated ions form water-insoluble carbonate (e.g., Na2CO3, CaCO3) \nclusters associated with permanent whitening of the surface [21]. In \nFig. 3, DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE laminates are \nillustrated after 0, 5,000 and 10,000h of DH exposure. Leaching induced \nsurface whitening was clearly discernible after 5,000h. The entire glass \nsurfaces of both laminates revealed a homogenous white appearance \nafter 10,000h. The surface whitening effect was not dependent on the \nencapsulant material (as shown in Fig. 3). \nMicroscopic images of surface polished EVA and POE laminates ob­\ntained by DLM (1st row) and PLM (2nd and 3rd row) after 0, 5,000, and \n10,000h of DH exposure are depicted and compared in Fig. 4. DLM \nimages revealed an increased whitening for both encapsulant types after \nFig. 2. Failure modes of compressive shear tested double-glass laminates: 1) interfacial failure at the encapsulant-glass interface, 2) cohesive failure: a) within the \nencapsulant, b) within the glass substrate. \nFig. 3. DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE-glass laminates \nexposed to DH for 0, 5,000 and 10,000h. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n4\n5,000h of DH exposure. The edge clearing effect was more pronounced \nfor EVA laminates. In contrast, the edges of the POE laminates appeared \nfully opaque after 10,000h (see Fig. 4). After 5,000h of DH exposure, \nspheric microdomains (maltese-crosses, d < 100 μm) were clearly \ndiscernible in the polarized light microscopic (PLM) images of both \nlaminate types (see Fig. 4). The microdomains differed in appearance, \nsize and number. Compared to POE, EVA laminates exhibited less, but \nlarger microdomains, which were presumably related to water uptake \nand clustering. The edge clearing effect of EVA laminates was dependent \non conditioning time at 23 ◦C and 50 %rh, subsequent to DH exposure. \nThe microdomains within the POE laminates were smaller and more \nhomogenous (see Fig. 4). Harris et al. [38] reported the formation of \ncalcite (CaCO3) crystals and spherolithic superstructures resulting in \nmaltese-crosses in PLM images. Most likely, the finer microdomains in \nPOE laminates could be attributed to glass ageing induced formation of \nCaCO3 and Na2CO3 crystals at the POE-glass interface [21]. Interest­\ningly, these microdomains were nucleated along the edge lines of the \npyramidal topography of the extruded POE film. In addition to the \nmaltese-cross microdomains, distinct black spots with a diameter up to \n2 mm were also discernible in POE laminates after 5,000 and 10,000h of \nDH exposure. \nIn contrast to POE, which was reported to prevent effectively ion \nmigration [17], the higher polarity of EVA favors ion diffusion into the \nbulk of the encapsulant [3,11,21]. Moreover, the damp heat induced \nhydrolysis of EVA could facilitate the formation of acetic acid, which \nwould reduce the alkalinity of the pH at the EVA-glass interface. These \ntwo mechanisms were ascribed to hinder the formation of sodium or \ncalcium carbonates at EVA-glass interfaces by keeping the pH value well \nbelow 9. \nSolar transmittance spectra of a single glass substrate and the lami­\nnates are depicted in Fig. 5. The hemispheric transmittance spectrum of \nthe glass substrate revealed an UV cut-off at 330 nm. The UV cut-off of \nEVA laminates shifted from 345 to 385 nm after 10,000h of damp heat \nexposure. A cut-off shift from 355 to 400 nm was also confirmed for POE \nlaminates. The results of the double-glass EVA laminates are in agree­\nment with findings in Refs. [35,39]. Morse et al. [39] did not detect any \nsignificant changes of the UV cut-off for POE laminates exposed to 1.5 \nMJ/m2 of solar radiation at 85 ◦C for 4,000h. The deviations within this \nwork and the findings in Ref. [39] were attributed to different ageing \nconditions. Barret et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat expo­\nsure on free standing crosslinked POE films and reported a decrease of \nthe hemispheric transmittance with increasing ageing durations, which \nis also in agreement with findings of this study (see Figs. 5 and 6b)). The \nlower hemispheric transmittance values of POE-based glass laminates in \nthe unaged reference state is presumably related to a stronger index of \nrefraction difference of glass and POE associated with higher reflectivity \nat the interfaces. The diffuse transmittance was significantly higher for \nthe unaged POE laminate. More pronounced scattering of POE is pre­\nsumably related to a higher degree of crystallinity and a lower cross­\nlinking density [19]. The increase in scattering at lower wavelength \nvalues is a clear indicator for scattering domains such as crystal lamellae \nwith a thickness well below 300 nm. While the lamellae thickness of PE \ncrystals is below 10 nm, the lateral dimensions of crystal lamellae are \nseveral 100 nm and hence, contributing to scattering [31]. Both lami­\nnates revealed hydrocarbon (CH) related absorption bands at 2500 to \n2200, 1800 to 1650, 1400 and 1200 nm and a small carbonyl (C=O) \nFig. 4. Direct (1st row) and polarized (2nd and 3rd row) light microscopic images of glass surface polished EVA (left) and POE (right) laminates exposed to DH for 0, \n5,000, and 10,000h. \nFig. 5. Effect of damp heat exposure (10,000h) on the hemispheric and diffuse transmittance (Th and Td) of EVA- and POE-glass laminates.  \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n5\npeak at 2100 nm [31]. Moreover, damp heat aged laminates (for 10, \n000h) revealed an additional band attributed to molecular water in the \nrange from 1950 to 1900 nm [31]. Interestingly, this band was more \npronounced for POE than for EVA laminates. Presumably, a water rich \nlayer was formed in the near interface glass regions of POE laminates \ndue to excellent bulk barrier properties of POE. However, water diffu­\nsion rates were reported to be at least two magnitudes higher at the \ninterface of metal oxides and polymeric adhesives, such as epoxies \n[40–42]. Presumably, the edges of the periodic, pyramidic topography \nresulted in imperfections at the interface allowing for fast ingress of \nwater. \nNormalized hemispheric transmittance values within the solar \n(300–2000 nm) and the UV (300–400 nm) range of EVA and POE lam­\ninates are illustrated in Fig. 6a). Unaged EVA laminates revealed slightly \nhigher (+1.5%) hemispheric solar and UV transmittance values than \nPOE. Compared to unaged laminates, damp heat exposed (1,000h) EVA \nand POE specimens exhibited a decrease in the hemispheric trans­\nmittance by −3.6 and −1.1%, respectively. After 10,000h of damp heat \nageing, the hemispheric solar transmittance was significantly higher for \nEVA laminates. A pronounced drop in solar transmittance (by −5.8%) \nwas ascertained just for the POE laminate. Morse et al. [39] reported a \ndecrease of hemispheric transmittance of double-glass EVA laminates by \n−3% due to global ageing at 80 ◦C, 30 %rh for 180 days. In contrast, no \nchanges were observed for POE laminates [39]. The poorer performance \nof POE laminates in this study was presumably attributable to damp heat \ninduced glass and interface ageing effects. A decrease of the hemispheric \ntransmittance was also described for free standing and damp heat \nexposed EVA and POE films [4]. \nUnexposed EVA laminates revealed about 4.0% higher hemispheric \nUV transmittance values than POE specimens (see Fig. 6a)). Damp heat \nexposure for 10,000h caused a significant decrease (−19.0%) of the UV \ntransmittance for both, EVA and POE laminates. \nThe solar haze increased by a factor of 9 after already 1,000h of \ndamp heat exposure of EVA laminates. No significant further changes \nwere discernible at longer damp heat exposure up to 10,000h. Compared \nto unaged specimens, POE laminates exhibited 2x (1,000h) and 3x \n(10,000h) higher haze values after damp heat exposure. However, the \nincrease in haze of POE-glass was much less compared to EVA-glass. The \nageing induced haze of the laminates wa"
outputs\codex_findings\m17_review\codex_stdout.log:4849:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_plan_pass3_stdout.txt:658:.\tests\e2e\live_integration_audit.py:1717:             "js": "(async () => { …252144 tokens truncated…ter-repellent fabrics, and industrial processes like metal plating.\n- Regulatory Challenges: The diversity of PFAS complicates regulation. Long-chain PFAS face stricter controls, while short-chain alternatives are under scrutiny. With over 4,000 PFAS compounds, comprehensive regulation is challenging, prompting a shift to group-based approaches.\nApplications and Uses of PFAS\nPFAS, known for their resilience and versatility, are extensively utilized in both industrial applications and consumer products, contributing to their pervasive presence in modern life.\nIndustrial Applications\nPFAS are widely used in industries due to their unique properties. They are critical in the manufacture of non-stick cookware, waterproof clothing, firefighting foams, and electronics. In the aerospace and automotive industries, PFAS are used for their ability to withstand extreme conditions, enhancing product performance and durability.\nConsumer Products Containing PFAS\nBeyond industrial use, PFAS are found in a vast array of consumer products. From stain-resistant carpets and water-repellent outdoor gear to food packaging and cosmetics, these chemicals are integral to many products that require durability and resistance to moisture or heat. Their widespread use has contributed to the global challenge of PFAS contamination.\nHealth and Environmental Impacts\nThe health and environmental impacts of PFAS are profound and widespread, raising significant concerns among scientists, regulators, and the public to reduce PFAS exposure and protect human health.\nHealth Effects of PFAS Exposure\nPFAS are known to accumulate in the human body over time, leading to potential health risks associated with both short-term and long-term exposure. These include:\n- Short-Term Health Effects: Short-term exposure to high levels of PFAS has been associated with various health issues, including skin rashes, liver damage, and immune system effects. While these effects are concerning, they are less documented compared to long-term risks.\n- Long-Term Health Risks: Chronic, low-level exposure to PFAS is more common and poses serious risks to human health. Epidemiological studies have linked long-term PFAS exposure to more serious health problems, including cancer, thyroid disorders, high cholesterol, and reproductive issues. The persistence of PFAS in the body, coupled with their ability to accumulate over time, makes them particularly dangerous.\nEnvironmental Impact of PFAS\nPFAS are often referred to as “forever chemicals” due to their extreme persistence in the environment.\nPFAS in Soil and Water\nContamination is a significant environmental concern, especially in water sources across industries. These chemicals are highly mobile, often leaching into groundwater and spreading far from their original source. Unregulated contaminant monitoring rules of water bodies can lead to widespread ecological disruption, affecting aquatic life and, subsequently, the food chain.\nBioaccumulation and Ecological Consequences\nPFAS tend to bioaccumulate, meaning they build up in the tissues of living organisms over time. This accumulation can lead to toxic effects on wildlife, including reproductive and developmental problems, and can disrupt entire ecosystems.\nThe Transcend Design Generator transforms the traditional approach to wastewater design with remarkable efficiency and precision. With the Transcend Design Generator, you can expect not only a faster design process but also superior outcomes that align with the latest standards in wastewater management.\nCase Studies: PFAS Contamination Incidents\nHigh-profile PFAS contamination incidents have exposed the widespread environmental and health risks associated with these chemicals, prompting legal action, regulatory scrutiny, and a reevaluation of preventive measures.\nThe Dark Waters of Parkersburg, WV\nDupont’s extensive use of PFOA (perfluorooctanoic acid) led to widespread contamination and significant health concerns among local residents. This case, documented in detail by reporter Nick Brumfield, has become a landmark example of corporate negligence and environmental harm.\nThe contamination, which began in the mid-20th century, was initially concealed by Dupont, despite mounting evidence linking PFOA to serious health issues such as cancer and liver damage. The coverup, driven by the company’s efforts to protect its profits and reputation, led to prolonged environmental degradation and personal suffering among affected communities.\nLessons Learned from These Incidents\nThe Dupont case underscores the critical need for transparency and accountability in environmental management. It also illustrates the broader implications of chemical contamination, urging a reevaluation of safety standards and regulatory measures to protect public health and the environment worldwide.\nRegulatory Landscape\nThe regulatory landscape for PFAS is evolving globally, with countries adopting varied approaches to address the environmental and health risks posed by these persistent chemicals.\nGlobal PFAS Regulations\nGlobal PFAS regulations are diverse, reflecting varying levels of awareness and response, with some countries enforcing stringent controls while others are still developing their regulatory frameworks.\nOverview of International Regulations\nCountries around the world have begun to recognize the dangers of PFAS, leading to a patchwork of regulations. Some countries, like Sweden and Germany, have taken a stringent approach, while others are still in the early stages of regulation.\nComparison of Regulatory Approaches by Country\nRegulatory approaches vary widely. The European Union (EU) has implemented some of the strictest controls, including restrictions on the use of certain PFAS in consumer products. In contrast, regulations in countries like the United States and China are more fragmented, with varying standards and a national PFAS testing strategy across states and provinces.\nPFAS Regulations in the United States\nIn the United States, PFAS regulations are shaped by a combination of federal guidelines from the EPA and more stringent state-specific initiatives aimed at addressing the growing concerns over PFAS contamination and public health.\nFederal Regulations and Guidelines\nAt the federal level, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has issued health advisories and is working on setting enforceable standards for PFAS in drinking water. The EPA’s PFAS Strategic Roadmap outlines a comprehensive plan to address PFAS contamination through regulation, cleanup, and research.\nState-Specific Regulations and Initiatives\nSeveral U.S. states, including Michigan, New Jersey, and California, have taken the lead in setting their own PFAS regulations, often stricter than federal guidelines. These state-level initiatives reflect growing concern over PFAS contamination and a desire to protect public health more aggressively.\nEuropean Union PFAS Regulations\nThe European Union has pioneered stringent PFAS regulations through comprehensive directives aiming to limit the use, release, and impact of these hazardous chemicals across various sectors.\nOverview of EU Regulatory Framework\nThe EU has been at the forefront of PFAS regulation, with several directives aimed at limiting the use and release of these chemicals. The REACH (Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation, and Restriction of Chemicals) regulation is a key component of the EU’s approach, targeting the most hazardous PFAS for restriction or phase-out.\nKey Directives and Their Implications\nKey directives include the EU’s Drinking Water Directive, which sets stringent limits on PFAS"
outputs\codex_findings\m17_review\codex_stdout.log:5826:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:716:      "content": "Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are a collection of synthetic chemicals that have existed since the 1940s. They’re famous for their remarkable resistance to water and grease, so they’re often called “forever chemicals.” With thousands of different compounds, their incredibly strong carbon-fluorine bonds make them persist in the environment for an extended time. In this article, Transcend will break down the evolution and impact of PFAS regulations over the years while suggesting innovative technology to assist the affected industries.\nHistorical Context and Origins\nPFAS were first developed in the mid-20th century during the post-war industrial boom. Companies like 3M and DuPont pioneered the use of PFAS in products like Teflon and Scotchgard, touting their remarkable resistance to heat, water, and oil. However, concerns about the environmental and health impacts of PFAS didn’t emerge until decades later, as these chemicals began to show up in water supplies, soils, and even human bloodstreams.\nTypes of PFAS\nPFAS are categorized into several subgroups, with the most well-known being perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS). Other PFAS types include fluorinated telomers, perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), and perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs).\nDifferences Between Various PFAS Compounds\nPFAS compounds are a large and diverse group of synthetic chemicals characterized by carbon-fluorine bonds, which impart high stability and resistance to degradation. Despite sharing these common characteristics, PFAS chemicals differ significantly in their molecular structure, leading to variations in their physical and chemical properties, environmental persistence, toxicity, and uses.\n- Chain Length: PFAS are categorized by carbon chain length: long-chain (e.g., PFOA, PFOS) and short-chain. Long-chain PFAS are more bioaccumulative and persistent, leading to stricter regulations. Short-chain PFAS are less bioaccumulative but still pose environmental risks.\n- Functional Groups: PFAS varies by functional group. Perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs), like PFOA and PFOS, are linked to contamination and health concerns. Fluoropolymers, used in non-stick coatings, are large molecules with lower direct risks.\n- Applications: Different structures lead to various uses, such as firefighting foams, water-repellent fabrics, and industrial processes like metal plating.\n- Regulatory Challenges: The diversity of PFAS complicates regulation. Long-chain PFAS face stricter controls, while short-chain alternatives are under scrutiny. With over 4,000 PFAS compounds, comprehensive regulation is challenging, prompting a shift to group-based approaches.\nApplications and Uses of PFAS\nPFAS, known for their resilience and versatility, are extensively utilized in both industrial applications and consumer products, contributing to their pervasive presence in modern life.\nIndustrial Applications\nPFAS are widely used in industries due to their unique properties. They are critical in the manufacture of non-stick cookware, waterproof clothing, firefighting foams, and electronics. In the aerospace and automotive industries, PFAS are used for their ability to withstand extreme conditions, enhancing product performance and durability.\nConsumer Products Containing PFAS\nBeyond industrial use, PFAS are found in a vast array of consumer products. From stain-resistant carpets and water-repellent outdoor gear to food packaging and cosmetics, these chemicals are integral to many products that require durability and resistance to moisture or heat. Their widespread use has contributed to the global challenge of PFAS contamination.\nHealth and Environmental Impacts\nThe health and environmental impacts of PFAS are profound and widespread, raising significant concerns among scientists, regulators, and the public to reduce PFAS exposure and protect human health.\nHealth Effects of PFAS Exposure\nPFAS are known to accumulate in the human body over time, leading to potential health risks associated with both short-term and long-term exposure. These include:\n- Short-Term Health Effects: Short-term exposure to high levels of PFAS has been associated with various health issues, including skin rashes, liver damage, and immune system effects. While these effects are concerning, they are less documented compared to long-term risks.\n- Long-Term Health Risks: Chronic, low-level exposure to PFAS is more common and poses serious risks to human health. Epidemiological studies have linked long-term PFAS exposure to more serious health problems, including cancer, thyroid disorders, high cholesterol, and reproductive issues. The persistence of PFAS in the body, coupled with their ability to accumulate over time, makes them particularly dangerous.\nEnvironmental Impact of PFAS\nPFAS are often referred to as “forever chemicals” due to their extreme persistence in the environment.\nPFAS in Soil and Water\nContamination is a significant environmental concern, especially in water sources across industries. These chemicals are highly mobile, often leaching into groundwater and spreading far from their original source. Unregulated contaminant monitoring rules of water bodies can lead to widespread ecological disruption, affecting aquatic life and, subsequently, the food chain.\nBioaccumulation and Ecological Consequences\nPFAS tend to bioaccumulate, meaning they build up in the tissues of living organisms over time. This accumulation can lead to toxic effects on wildlife, including reproductive and developmental problems, and can disrupt entire ecosystems.\nThe Transcend Design Generator transforms the traditional approach to wastewater design with remarkable efficiency and precision. With the Transcend Design Generator, you can expect not only a faster design process but also superior outcomes that align with the latest standards in wastewater management.\nCase Studies: PFAS Contamination Incidents\nHigh-profile PFAS contamination incidents have exposed the widespread environmental and health risks associated with these chemicals, prompting legal action, regulatory scrutiny, and a reevaluation of preventive measures.\nThe Dark Waters of Parkersburg, WV\nDupont’s extensive use of PFOA (perfluorooctanoic acid) led to widespread contamination and significant health concerns among local residents. This case, documented in detail by reporter Nick Brumfield, has become a landmark example of corporate negligence and environmental harm.\nThe contamination, which began in the mid-20th century, was initially concealed by Dupont, despite mounting evidence linking PFOA to serious health issues such as cancer and liver damage. The coverup, driven by the company’s efforts to protect its profits and reputation, led to prolonged environmental degradation and personal suffering among affected communities.\nLessons Learned from These Incidents\nThe Dupont case underscores the critical need for transparency and accountability in environmental management. It also illustrates the broader implications of chemical contamination, urging a reevaluation of safety standards and regulatory measures to protect public health and the environment worldwide.\nRegulatory Landscape\nThe regulatory landscape for PFAS is evolving globally, with countries adopting varied approaches to address the environmental and health risks posed by these persistent chemicals.\nGlobal PFAS Regulations\nGlobal PFAS regulations are diverse, reflecting varying levels of awareness and response, with some countries enforcing stringent controls while others are still developing their regulatory frameworks.\nOverview of International Regulations\nCountries around the world have begun to recognize the dangers of PFAS, leading to a patchwork of regulations. Some countries, like Sweden and Germany, have taken a stringent approach, while others are still in the early stages of regulation.\nComparison of Regulatory Approaches by Country\nRegulatory approaches vary widely. The European Union (EU) has implemented some of the strictest controls, including restrictions on the use of certain PFAS in consumer products. In contrast, regulations in countries like the United States and China are more fragmented, with varying standards and a national PFAS testing strategy across states and provinces.\nPFAS Regulations in the United States\nIn the United States, PFAS regulations are shaped by a combination of federal guidelines from the EPA and more stringent state-specific initiatives aimed at addressing the growing concerns over PFAS contamination and public health.\nFederal Regulations and Guidelines\nAt the federal level, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has issued health advisories and is working on setting enforceable standards for PFAS in drinking water. The EPA’s PFAS Strategic Roadmap outlines a comprehensive plan to address PFAS contamination through regulation, cleanup, and research.\nState-Specific Regulations and Initiatives\nSeveral U.S. states, including Michigan, New Jersey, and California, have taken the lead in setting their own PFAS regulations, often stricter than federal guidelines. These state-level initiatives reflect growing concern over PFAS contamination and a desire to protect public health more aggressively.\nEuropean Union PFAS Regulations\nThe European Union has pioneered stringent PFAS regulations through comprehensive directives aiming to limit the use, release, and impact of these hazardous chemicals across various sectors.\nOverview of EU Regulatory Framework\nThe EU has been at the forefront of PFAS regulation, with several directives aimed at limiting the use and release of these chemicals. The REACH (Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation, and Restriction of Chemicals) regulation is a key component of the EU’s approach, targeting the most hazardous PFAS for restriction or phase-out.\nKey Directives and Their Implications\nKey directives include the EU’s Drinking Water Directive, which sets stringent limits on PFAS"

This accumulation can lead to toxic effects on wildlife, including reproductive and developmental problems, and can disrupt entire ecosystems.\nThe Transcend Design Generator transforms the traditional approach to wastewater design with remarkable efficiency and precision. With the Transcend Design Generator, you can expect not only a faster design process but also superior outcomes that align with the latest standards in wastewater management.\nCase Studies: PFAS Contamination Incidents\nHigh-profile PFAS contamination incidents have exposed the widespread environmental and health risks associated with these chemicals, prompting legal action, regulatory scrutiny, and a reevaluation of preventive measures.\nThe Dark Waters of Parkersburg, WV\nDupont’s extensive use of PFOA (perfluorooctanoic acid) led to widespread contamination and significant health concerns among local residents. This case, documented in detail by reporter Nick Brumfield, has become a landmark example of corporate negligence and environmental harm.\nThe contamination, which began in the mid-20th century, was initially concealed by Dupont, despite mounting evidence linking PFOA to serious health issues such as cancer and liver damage. The coverup, driven by the company’s efforts to protect its profits and reputation, led to prolonged environmental degradation and personal suffering among affected communities.\nLessons Learned from These Incidents\nThe Dupont case underscores the critical need for transparency and accountability in environmental management. It also illustrates the broader implications of chemical contamination, urging a reevaluation of safety standards and regulatory measures to protect public health and the environment worldwide.\nRegulatory Landscape\nThe regulatory landscape for PFAS is evolving globally, with countries adopting varied approaches to address the environmental and health risks posed by these persistent chemicals.\nGlobal PFAS Regulations\nGlobal PFAS regulations are diverse, reflecting varying levels of awareness and response, with some countries enforcing stringent controls while others are still developing their regulatory frameworks.\nOverview of International Regulations\nCountries around the world have begun to recognize the dangers of PFAS, leading to a patchwork of regulations. Some countries, like Sweden and Germany, have taken a stringent approach, while others are still in the early stages of regulation.\nComparison of Regulatory Approaches by Country\nRegulatory approaches vary widely. The European Union (EU) has implemented some of the strictest controls, including restrictions on the use of certain PFAS in consumer products. In contrast, regulations in countries like the United States and China are more fragmented, with varying standards and a national PFAS testing strategy across states and provinces.\nPFAS Regulations in the United States\nIn the United States, PFAS regulations are shaped by a combination of federal guidelines from the EPA and more stringent state-specific initiatives aimed at addressing the growing concerns over PFAS contamination and public health.\nFederal Regulations and Guidelines\nAt the federal level, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has issued health advisories and is working on setting enforceable standards for PFAS in drinking water. The EPA’s PFAS Strategic Roadmap outlines a comprehensive plan to address PFAS contamination through regulation, cleanup, and research.\nState-Specific Regulations and Initiatives\nSeveral U.S. states, including Michigan, New Jersey, and California, have taken the lead in setting their own PFAS regulations, often stricter than federal guidelines. These state-level initiatives reflect growing concern over PFAS contamination and a desire to protect public health more aggressively.\nEuropean Union PFAS Regulations\nThe European Union has pioneered stringent PFAS regulations through comprehensive directives aiming to limit the use, release, and impact of these hazardous chemicals across various sectors.\nOverview of EU Regulatory Framework\nThe EU has been at the forefront of PFAS regulation, with several directives aimed at limiting the use and release of these chemicals. The REACH (Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation, and Restriction of Chemicals) regulation is a key component of the EU’s approach, targeting the most hazardous PFAS for restriction or phase-out.\nKey Directives and Their Implications\nKey directives include the EU’s Drinking Water Directive, which sets stringent limits on PFAS"

# I-carney-005 — POLARIS webui (Next.js 16) production image.
#
# Multi-stage build: deps → builder → runner with .next/standalone output.
# Per memory next_16_breaking_changes.md, Next 16 has standalone output that
# bundles only the minimum runtime deps; runner stage is tiny.
#
# Codex diff iter-1+2 P1-B: NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL is intentionally NOT a
# build arg. Baking it would freeze an absolute URL (e.g., http://api:8000,
# a Docker service hostname) into the client bundle and break production
# browsers. Instead:
#   - web/lib/api.ts hard-codes `/api/v6` for browser-relative fetch.
#   - INTERNAL_API_URL (server-side only) is a BUILD ARG; Next.js evaluates
#     rewrites() destinations during `next build` and bakes them into the
#     standalone routes manifest, so this MUST be set at build time. NOT
#     prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_, so it does NOT leak into the client bundle.

# ----------- Stage 1: deps -----------
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --no-audit --no-fund

# ----------- Stage 2: builder -----------
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
# Codex diff iter-2 P1-B (continuing): Next.js evaluates rewrites()
# DESTINATIONS at build time and bakes them into the routes manifest.
# INTERNAL_API_URL must therefore be a build arg, not a runtime env. It is
# NOT prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_, so it does NOT leak into the client bundle
# — only the server-side route table sees it. Compose sets the build arg to
# `http://api:8000` via build.args.INTERNAL_API_URL.
ARG INTERNAL_API_URL=http://api:8000
ENV INTERNAL_API_URL=${INTERNAL_API_URL}
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# ----------- Stage 3: runner -----------
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV PORT=3000
# Standalone output: contains a minimal server.js + only the deps needed
# to run it. Requires output: 'standalone' in next.config.ts (P1-004).
COPY --from=builder /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public

EXPOSE 3000

# Healthcheck via wget --spider (busybox alpine built-in).
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=20s --retries=3 \
    CMD wget --spider -q http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

 succeeded in 1616ms:

  default_action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.webui.arn
  }
}

# Codex diff iter-1 P1-001: I-carney-005 has the frontend call /api/v6/*
# (browser-relative) which Next.js then REWRITES server-side to api:8000/*
# (the FastAPI surface does NOT serve /api/v6 prefix). Routing /api/v6/*
# directly at the ALB to FastAPI would 404 because FastAPI sees /api/v6/runs
# not /runs. So the only listener rule we need at the ALB is sending
# EVERYTHING to webui; Next.js handles the rewrite + SSE pass-through.
#
# Operator /health probe goes direct to the api target group (skipping
# Next.js) for clean liveness signals.
resource "aws_lb_listener_rule" "health" {
  listener_arn = aws_lb_listener.https.arn
  priority     = 10

  action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.api.arn
  }

  condition {
    path_pattern {
      values = ["/health"]
    }
  }
}

# ----- HTTP → 301 HTTPS redirect ----- #

resource "aws_lb_listener" "http_redirect" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.polaris.arn
  port              = 80
  protocol          = "HTTP"

  default_action {
    type = "redirect"
    redirect {
      protocol    = "HTTPS"
      port        = "443"
      status_code = "HTTP_301"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_wafv2_web_acl" "polaris" {
  name  = "polaris-carney"
  scope = "REGIONAL"

  default_action {
    allow {}
  }

  rule {
    name     = "AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet"
    priority = 1
    override_action {
      none {}
    }
    statement {
      managed_rule_group_statement {
        name        = "AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet"
        vendor_name = "AWS"
      }
    }
    visibility_config {
      cloudwatch_metrics_enabled = true
      metric_name                = "common-rule-set"
      sampled_requests_enabled   = true
    }
  }

  rule {
    name     = "AWSManagedRulesKnownBadInputsRuleSet"
    priority = 2
    override_action {
      none {}
    }
    statement {
      managed_rule_group_statement {
        name        = "AWSManagedRulesKnownBadInputsRuleSet"
        vendor_name = "AWS"
      }
    }
    visibility_config {
      cloudwatch_metrics_enabled = true
      metric_name                = "known-bad-inputs"
      sampled_requests_enabled   = true
    }
  }

  visibility_config {
    cloudwatch_metrics_enabled = true
    metric_name                = "polaris-carney-acl"
    sampled_requests_enabled   = true
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "alb_logs" {
  bucket = "polaris-carney-alb-logs-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
  # Demo-window only; let terraform destroy clean up.
  force_destroy = true
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "alb_logs" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.id

  rule {
    id     = "expire-30d"
    status = "Enabled"
    filter {} # empty filter = match all objects
    expiration {
      days = 30
    }
  }
}

# Allow the regional AWS Elastic Load Balancing service account to write logs.
# ca-central-1 ELB log delivery account: 985666609251 (per AWS docs as of 2026).
data "aws_iam_policy_document" "alb_logs_bucket" {
  statement {
    actions   = ["s3:PutObject"]
    resources = ["${aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.arn}/alb/AWSLogs/${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}/*"]

    principals {
      type        = "AWS"
      identifiers = ["arn:aws:iam::985666609251:root"]
    }
  }

  statement {
    actions   = ["s3:GetBucketAcl"]
    resources = [aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.arn]
    principals {
      type        = "Service"
      identifiers = ["delivery.logs.amazonaws.com"]
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "alb_logs" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.id
  policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.alb_logs_bucket.json
}
